questions.question: What is the approximate age of the universe according to the Big Bang theory?
questions.explanation: The universe is very old – almost 20 billion years old.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: When was the Earth estimated to have been formed?
questions.explanation: Earth was supposed to have been formed about 4.5 billion years back.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: The 'Big Bang' theory attempts to explain the origin of:
questions.explanation: The Big Bang theory attempts to explain to us the origin of universe.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: According to early Greek thinkers, units of life called ______ were transferred to different planets including Earth.
questions.explanation: Early Greek thinkers thought units of life called spores were transferred to different planets including earth.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Who experimentally demonstrated that life comes only from pre-existing life, dismissing the theory of spontaneous generation?
questions.explanation: Louis Pasteur by careful experimentation demonstrated that life comes only from pre-existing life.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Oparin and Haldane proposed that the first form of life could have come from:
questions.explanation: Oparin and Haldane proposed that the first form of life could have come from pre-existing non-living organic molecules (e.g. RNA, protein, etc.).
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: In 1953, S.L. Miller created similar conditions of the primitive Earth in a laboratory, which included high temperature and which gas mixture?
options: CH4, H2, NH3 and water vapour
questions.explanation: Miller created electric discharge in a closed flask containing CH4, H2, NH3 and water vapour at 800°C.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: What was the specific observation made by S.L. Miller in his experiment after creating primitive Earth conditions?
questions.explanation: He observed formation of amino acids in his experiment.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: The first non-cellular forms of life could have originated ________ years ago.
questions.explanation: The first non-cellular forms of life could have originated 3 billion years back.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: The first cellular forms of life probably did not possibly appear until ________ years ago.
questions.explanation: The first cellular form of life did not possibly originate till about 2000 million (2 billion) years ago.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: What is the basis of the theory of Natural Selection proposed by Charles Darwin?
questions.explanation: Fitness, according to Darwin, refers ultimately and only to reproductive fitness.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Paleontological evidence for evolution refers to the study of:
questions.explanation: Fossils are remains of hard parts of life-forms found in rocks... The study of fossils in different sedimentary layers indicates the geological period in which they existed.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: The presence of homologous structures in different organisms indicates:
questions.explanation: Homology indicates common ancestry and is based on divergent evolution.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of homologous organs?
options: Forelimbs of whales, bats, cheetah and human
questions.explanation: Whales, bats, cheetah and human share similarities in the pattern of bones of forelimbs.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Analogy (Analogous structures) represents:
questions.explanation: Analogy is the result of convergent evolution – different structures evolving for the same function.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Sweet potato (root modification) and potato (stem modification) are examples of:
questions.explanation: Sweet potato and potato are examples of analogy as they perform the same function (storage) but have different origins.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Industrial melanism observed in the peppered moth (Biston betularia) in England is an example of:
questions.explanation: The change in moth population proportion after industrialization is an example of Natural Selection.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Evolution by 'anthropogenic action' refers to evolution caused by:
questions.explanation: Anthropogenic action refers to human-caused changes, such as use of herbicides, pesticides, or antibiotics.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: The process of evolution of different species in a given geographical area starting from a point and radiating to other areas of geography is called:
questions.explanation: This process of evolution... is called adaptive radiation.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Which of the following is a classic example of adaptive radiation?
questions.explanation: Darwin's Finches represent one of the best examples of adaptive radiation.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: According to Lamarck, evolution of life forms occurred driven by:
questions.explanation: Lamarck said that evolution of life forms had occurred but driven by use and disuse of organs.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: The two key concepts of Darwinian Theory of Evolution are Branching Descent and:
questions.explanation: Branching descent and natural selection are the two key concepts of Darwinian Theory of Evolution.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Who proposed the 'Mutation Theory' based on work on evening primrose?
questions.explanation: Hugo de Vries based on his work on evening primrose brought forth the idea of mutations.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: De Vries used the term ________ to describe single-step large mutations.
questions.explanation: Hugo de Vries believed mutation caused speciation and hence called it saltation (single step large mutation).
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: The Hardy-Weinberg principle states that allele frequencies in a population are stable and constant from generation to generation in the absence of:
questions.explanation: Hardy-Weinberg principle says that allele frequencies in a population are stable and is constant from generation to generation.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the five factors known to affect Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium?
questions.explanation: Five factors are gene migration, genetic drift, mutation, genetic recombination and natural selection.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: When the original drifted population becomes founders and the change in allele frequency is so different that they form a new species, it is called:
questions.explanation: The original drifted population becomes founders and the effect is called founder effect.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: In Natural Selection, if more individuals acquire peripheral character value at both ends of the distribution curve, it is called:
questions.explanation: Disruptive selection occurs when more individuals acquire peripheral character value at both ends of the distribution curve.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Which type of natural selection leads to the mean character value being strengthened?
questions.explanation: In stabilising selection, more individuals acquire mean character value.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: The first organisms that invaded land were:
questions.explanation: Plants were the first organisms to colonise land.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Fish with stout and strong fins that could move on land and go back to water were called:
questions.explanation: In 1938, a fish caught in South Africa happened to be a Coelacanth which was thought to be extinct. These animals called lobe fins evolved into the first amphibians.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Which of the following evolved into the first amphibians?
questions.explanation: Lobe fins evolved into the first amphibians that lived on both land and water.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: The 'Age of Reptiles' refers to which geological era?
questions.explanation: Giant ferns (pteridophytes) were present but they all fell to form coal deposits slowly... In the next 200 million years or so, reptiles of different shapes and sizes dominated on earth.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Which was the biggest dinosaur, reaching about 20 feet in height?
questions.explanation: The biggest of them, i.e., Tyrannosaurus rex was about 20 feet in height and had huge fearsome dagger like teeth.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: About how many million years ago did dinosaurs suddenly disappear from the Earth?
questions.explanation: About 65 mya, the dinosaurs suddenly disappeared from the earth.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: The first mammals were like:
questions.explanation: The first mammals were like shrews.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Dryopithecus and Ramapithecus existed about ________ years ago.
questions.explanation: About 15 mya, primates called Dryopithecus and Ramapithecus were existing.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Which primate was more man-like between Dryopithecus and Ramapithecus?
questions.explanation: Ramapithecus was more man-like while Dryopithecus was more ape-like.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Australopithecines probably lived in the:
questions.explanation: Evidence shows they probably lived in East African grasslands.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Which hominid was the first 'human-like' being with a brain capacity between 650-800cc?
questions.explanation: The first human-like being the hominid was called Homo habilis. The brain capacities were between 650-800cc.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Homo erectus fossils discovered in Java in 1891 revealed a brain capacity of around:
questions.explanation: Homo erectus had a large brain around 900cc.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Neanderthal man lived in near East and Central Asia between ________ years ago.
questions.explanation: The Neanderthal man... lived in near east and central Asia between 1,00,000-40,000 years back.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: The brain capacity of Neanderthal man was approximately:
questions.explanation: Neanderthal man had a brain size of 1400cc.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Homo sapiens arose in ________ and moved across continents.
questions.explanation: Homo sapiens arose in Africa and moved across continents and developed into distinct races.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: During the last ice age between 75,000-10,000 years ago, which human species arose?
questions.explanation: During ice age between 75,000-10,000 years ago modern Homo sapiens arose.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Pre-historic cave art developed about ________ years ago.
questions.explanation: Pre-historic cave art developed about 18,000 years ago.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Agriculture and human settlements came into existence around ________ years ago.
questions.explanation: Agriculture came around 10,000 years back and human settlements started.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Darwin's finches showing different beak structures based on food sources is an example of evolution by:
questions.explanation: Finches evolved from original seed-eating features to insectivorous and vegetarian finches through adaptation to new habitats.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Embryological support for evolution was proposed by Ernst Heckel but later disapproved by:
questions.explanation: This proposal (Embryological support) was disapproved on careful study performed by Karl Ernst von Baer.
questions.subject: Biology

questions.question: Who worked in Malay Archipelago and reached similar conclusions as Darwin regarding evolution?
questions.explanation: A naturalist Alfred Wallace who worked in Malay Archipelago had also come to similar conclusions around the same time as Darwin.
questions.subject: Biology
